title: Langues de chat
subtitle: French cat tongue biscuits: thin, crisp wafers piped from a stiff butter batter
excerpt: Thin, crisp, finger-length French biscuits made from a stiff batter of butter, icing sugar, egg whites, and flour piped into long narrow strips. The name (cat tongues) describes the shape. They bake quickly at moderate heat to pale golden with slightly darker edges. Served alongside mousses, ice creams, and coffee. Simple to make once the piping technique is established.
sourceNotes: Langues de chat are a classical French patisserie preparation documented in nineteenth-century cookery literature. Jules Gouffé, Le Livre de Pâtisserie (1873, Project Gutenberg), gives a recipe for cat tongue biscuits consistent with the modern butter-and-egg-white batter piped into long strips. Auguste Escoffier, Le Guide Culinaire (1903, Project Gutenberg), lists them as petit four sec, noting they are piped and baked in minutes. The recipe and technique have remained unchanged in professional French patisserie since.

content.text: The batter is made quickly; most of the effort is in
. Use a cool batter (refrigerate it for 10 minutes if it feels too soft after mixing) and apply steady, even pressure. These biscuits bake fast: check from 8 minutes.

Heat the oven to 190°C conventional (170°C fan). Line two trays with baking paper. Beat the softened butter and icing sugar together until pale and fluffy. Add the vanilla and salt. Add the egg whites one at a time, beating well between each addition; the batter may look slightly curdled at this point. Fold in the sifted flour until just combined. The batter should be smooth, thick, and just piping-soft.

Transfer to a piping bag fitted with a plain 8mm round tip. Pipe strips about 6 to 7 cm long onto the lined trays, spacing them at least 3 cm apart. Hold the bag at a 45-degree angle and move backward as you apply pressure. Bake for 8 to 12 minutes until the biscuits are pale gold with lightly browned edges. The centre should feel set, not soft. Transfer to a rack immediately; they crisp up as they cool. Serve alongside ice cream, mousse, or coffee.
